items.q: Why is Jupiter classified as a gas giant?
items.correct: Its bulk is mostly hydrogen and helium with no defined solid surface.
items.misconception: It is mostly air like Earth's atmosphere.
items.why: Students project Earth's atmosphere onto Jupiter. Jupiter has no rocky surface - it's gas transitioning to metallic hydrogen with depth. Very different from a thin atmosphere around a solid planet.

items.q: What does cosmological redshift tell us?
items.correct: Galaxies are receding from us due to the expansion of space.
items.misconception: Galaxies are turning red as they age.
items.random_wrong: Light gets tired and loses energy.
items.why: Students take redshift literally as colour change. It's a WAVELENGTH STRETCH - light waves get longer because space itself expands while light travels, shifting visible light toward the red end of the spectrum.

items.q: What is the 'habitable zone' around a star?
items.correct: The region where liquid water could exist on a planet's surface.
items.random_wrong: Where humans could survive without spacesuits.
items.why: Students assume habitable zone = inhabited or human-suitable. It's a NECESSARY but not sufficient condition for Earth-like life - based purely on temperature for liquid water; doesn't guarantee atmosphere or life.

items.q: Length of the solar cycle (sunspot count)?
items.why: It's 11 years. “1 year” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Charged particles streaming from the Sun are called?
items.why: It's Solar wind. “Solar flares” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Length of the sunspot cycle in years?
items.why: The real figure is 11 years. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
